text: You delivered the talk to a live audience. Now the same work must reach readers.
On the page, we do not say I think. We say the results suggest.

Patricia, the manuscript looks great. Is the abstract ready for submission?
Almost. I am checking the tenses. The methods are in the past simple, and the findings are in the present perfect.
Good. And the citations -- have you added the recent ones?
Yes. Every claim now has a citation. The study has shown the effect, and the references support it.
Perfect. Which journal are we sending it to, and when does peer review start?
The leading clinical journal. Once I finish the abstract, I will send the submission, and peer review begins.
The abstract sums up the aim, the methods and the findings.
We measured recovery over six months.
Every claim is backed by a citation.

Good afternoon. Let me walk you through the abstract of our new manuscript before we submit it to the journal. An abstract is short, but it has a clear shape, and every sentence does one job. The first sentence gives the background and the gap: little is known about how the supplement affects recovery in older patients. The second sentence states the aim and the hypothesis: we predicted that a higher dose would lead to a better outcome. Then come the methods, written in the past simple, because they are finished: we recruited ninety patients, we measured their recovery over six months, and we analyzed the data. After the methods come the findings, and here the tense changes. We use the present perfect, because the findings are new and still matter today: the study has shown a strong association between the dose and recovery, and we have found that the effect is significant above a clear threshold. The final sentence gives the conclusion and why it matters: these results have implications for how the supplement is prescribed, and they support a larger trial. Notice the register. There is no I think and no maybe. We write we found, the data show, the results suggest. Every claim is careful, and every claim is backed by the findings. Once the abstract is clear, the rest of the paper, and the peer review, become much easier.

Hi Patricia, this is the handling editor at the journal, calling about your submission. Good news first: the reviewers have read your manuscript and the response is positive. They describe the findings as solid and the writing as clear. I have two requests before we move forward. First, please check your tenses in the abstract. In a few places you have used the past simple for the findings, but our style asks for the present perfect there, because the findings still stand. So change we found a strong association to we have found a strong association. Keep the past simple for the methods, that part is correct. Second, the reviewers have asked for two more citations in the introduction, to show how your work builds on earlier research. Add those, and add the reference to the citation list at the end. One reviewer has also suggested a small change to the hypothesis sentence, to make the prediction sharper. I will send the full comments by email today. If you return the revised manuscript within three weeks, we should be able to confirm acceptance for the next issue. Congratulations again, Patricia. This is strong work, and we are glad to have it. Talk soon.

First, give the background and the gap your study fills. Next, state the aim and the hypothesis you tested. After that, describe the methods in the past simple. Then, report the findings in the present perfect. Finally, give the conclusion and explain why it matters.
